name: Dian Cécht
description: Dian Cécht, whose name translates roughly to "Force of Healing," is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, primarily associated with healing, medicine, and magic. He is the father of several notable figures, including Miach, Airmed, and Etan, all of whom possess skills in healing and medicine. Dian Cécht is often depicted as a skilled physician and a powerful magician, capable of performing miraculous feats of healing and even resurrection. He is a member of the Tuatha Dé Danann, the race of gods and goddesses who inhabited Ireland before the arrival of the Gaels. His abilities extend beyond simple healing; he is known for crafting magical objects and creating powerful potions. His most famous creation is the Well of Sláine, a magical well that could restore the dead to life, though only if their heads were still attached. Dian Cécht's story is often intertwined with themes of jealousy and competition, particularly with his son Miach, whose healing abilities surpassed his own. Despite his contributions to the Tuatha Dé Danann, he is sometimes portrayed as flawed, driven by pride and ambition. He played a crucial role in the various battles and conflicts of the Tuatha Dé Danann, providing medical assistance and magical support to his people. His legacy is one of both great healing and tragic consequences, highlighting the complexities of divine power and familial relationships within Irish mythology. He is a powerful figure revered for his healing prowess and role within the Tuatha Dé Danann.
subtitle: God of Healing and Medicine

keyMyths.title: The Well of Sláine
keyMyths.description: Dian Cécht created the Well of Sláine, a magical well that could revive the dead. It was used to heal the Tuatha Dé Danann during battles.

keyMyths.title: Miach's Healing
keyMyths.description: Dian Cécht was jealous of his son Miach's superior healing abilities. He eventually killed Miach out of envy.

keyMyths.title: Nuada's Silver Arm
keyMyths.description: Dian Cécht crafted a silver arm for Nuada after the king lost his arm in battle, allowing him to regain his kingship.
